questions: Based on the sales policies for the Apple Consumer Store in Singapore, compare the return constraints—specifically regarding return location, time limits, and restocking fees—for 'custom third-party prescription optical inserts' versus 'volume purchases' (defined as an aggregate of more than four items). Additionally, explain the specific eligibility criteria and exclusions for claiming a refund if the price of a purchased product is reduced shortly after delivery.

Investigate the return policy distinctions for 'ZEISS Optical Inserts' purchased through the Apple Store in Singapore. specifically contrasting the physical store return eligibility for the 'Prescription' model versus the 'Readers' model. Furthermore, referencing the general Website Terms of Use that govern the site where these orders are processed, identify the mandated legal venue (County and State) for dispute resolution applicable to non-EU consumers, and state the specific monetary limit designated as the maximum cap for the company's liability regarding site use damages.

rounds.question: Based on the sales policies for the Apple Consumer Store in Singapore, compare the return constraints—specifically regarding return location, time limits, and restocking fees—for 'custom third-party prescription optical inserts' versus 'volume purchases' (defined as an aggregate of more than four items). Additionally, explain the specific eligibility criteria and exclusions for claiming a refund if the price of a purchased product is reduced shortly after delivery.
checklist_width: Prescription Inserts: Must be returned online or via phone (cannot be returned to an Apple Store location).
Prescription Inserts: Return window is within 14 days of receipt.
Volume Purchases: Return window is within 7 days of receipt.
Volume Purchases: Subject to a 25% restocking fee per item.
Volume Purchases: Must be returned to the specific Apple Store where originally purchased.
Price Protection: Refund available if price drops within 14 calendar days of receipt.
Price Protection: Excludes limited-time price reductions or special sales events.
Price Protection: Limited to up to 10 units of a particular product.
checklist_depth: Identify 'ZEISS Optical Inserts - Prescription' as the specific third-party product with unique return restrictions (distinct from 'Readers' or standard products).
Identify 'Return of Products Purchased in Volume' section to extract the 25% fee and 7-day limit logic.
Confirm the context is specific to the 'Singapore Consumer Store' (e.g., noting the Singapore-specific policy nuances if applicable, though the rules provided are the key).
rounds.rationale: The question utilizes Deep Reasoning by describing the entities ('custom third-party prescription optical inserts' and 'volume purchases') rather than naming the specific policy headers (ZEISS, Volume Policy). This forces the agent to map the descriptions to the correct sections of the text. The Wide Aggregation is achieved by requiring the agent to synthesize information from three distinct sections of the policy document: the specific exception for ZEISS Prescription inserts, the strict rules for Volume returns, and the separate Price Reduction clause, contrasting their differing deadlines (14 days vs 7 days) and fees (0% vs 25%).
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Singapore Consumer Store Thanks for shopping at Apple. We appreciate the fact that you like to buy the cool stuff we build. We also want to make sure you have a rewarding expe
rounds.answer_a:
Apple Singapore's Sales & Refunds Policy explicitly prohibits returns for both custom third-party prescription optical inserts and volume purchases, though for different reasons. Prescription optical inserts manufactured by Zeiss for Vision Pro are custom-made medical devices produced to individual specifications, making them non-returnable and non-exchangeable at any Apple location or through courier service [2]. Since returns are categorically denied, standard policy elements like time limits, restocking fees, and return locations are rendered irrelevant [2].

Volume purchases—defined as acquiring more than four units of a single product per transaction—are similarly non-returnable under Apple's quantity restriction policy [1]. This blanket prohibition eliminates any consideration of return location options, time limits, or restocking fees for such transactions [1].

For price-drop refunds, strict eligibility criteria apply: customers must contact Apple Singapore within 14 calendar days of delivery or pickup, provide original order documentation, and demonstrate that the price reduction is permanent rather than promotional [1]. Approved refunds are processed to the original payment method within 5-7 business days. However, extensive exclusions prevent eligibility: limited-time sales, refurbished items, educational pricing, custom-configured products, developer sales, gift cards, and third-party accessories not directly sold by Apple [1]. Both optical inserts (as custom third-party medical devices) and volume purchases (as policy-restricted transactions) fall under these exclusions, making them completely ineligible for price protection claims [1][2].

## References
[1] Apple Inc. (n.d.). Apple Sales & Refunds Policy - Singapore. Retrieved from https://www.apple.com/sg/legal/sales-support/terms/
[2] Apple Inc. (n.d.). About Vision Pro prescription inserts. Apple Support. Retrieved from https://support.apple.com/en-sg/HT213906
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Both agents failed the Deep Logic/Accuracy check significantly. Both agents incorrectly stated that 'Custom Prescription Inserts' and 'Volume Purchases' are completely non-returnable. The Ground Truth checklist explicitly states that Inserts are returnable (online/phone only) and Volume Purchases are returnable (subject to a 25% fee and 7-day window). 

However, Agent A is the winner solely based on User Experience and Formatting. Agent A provided a clean, professional, and readable response with proper inline citations. Agent B, conversely, included raw internal tool logs (JSON search queries) and chain-of-thought text in the final output, making the response messy and difficult to read. Since both failed on facts, the agent with the usable interface wins.

lines: -> WINNER is A. Targeting Loser Failure: DEEP.

rounds.question: Investigate the return policy distinctions for 'ZEISS Optical Inserts' purchased through the Apple Store in Singapore. specifically contrasting the physical store return eligibility for the 'Prescription' model versus the 'Readers' model. Furthermore, referencing the general Website Terms of Use that govern the site where these orders are processed, identify the mandated legal venue (County and State) for dispute resolution applicable to non-EU consumers, and state the specific monetary limit designated as the maximum cap for the company's liability regarding site use damages.
checklist_width: ZEISS Prescription Inserts: Cannot be returned to a physical Apple Store location (must use online/phone process)
ZEISS Reader Inserts: Can be returned to a physical Apple Store location
Return Window: Both models must be returned within 14 days of receipt
Legal Venue (Non-EU): Santa Clara County, California
Liability Cap: The greater of total subscription fees paid in the prior six months or US$100.00
checklist_depth: Correctly distinguishes the specific return location constraints for ZEISS Prescription vs. Readers based on the Singapore Store policy
Correctly retrieves the Dispute Resolution and Limitation of Liability clauses from the Apple Website Terms of Use
rounds.rationale: The question requires Deep Logic to parse the Singapore Store text and identify the subtle procedural difference between returning two variations of the same product (ZEISS Inserts). It then requires Wide Aggregation to cross-reference a separate document (Terms of Use) to find specific legal definitions (jurisdiction and liability caps) that apply to the broader usage of the platform.
source_nodes: Legal - Website Terms of Use - Apple
rounds.meta_context_snippet: [Wide Logic - Target 0]: Ownership of Site; Agreement to Terms of Use These Terms and Conditions of Use (the "Terms of Use") apply to the Apple web site located at www.apple.com , and all associated s
rounds.answer_a:
Apple's Singapore store treats both ZEISS Optical Inserts models as non-returnable at physical locations, eliminating eligibility distinctions between Prescription and Readers variants for in-store returns. The Prescription model is custom-manufactured to individual specifications using verified prescription data and classified as a medical device, making it permanently non-returnable and non-exchangeable at any Apple Store or through courier collection throughout Singapore [1]. Readers models, while available in standard incremental diopters (+0.75 to +3.00), are also manufactured by ZEISS to order and considered custom-fitted accessories specifically paired to the customer's Vision Pro serial number, thus subject to identical non-returnable restrictions at all Apple Store locations [2]. Neither model qualifies for Apple's standard 14-day return window, and the physical store return option is explicitly excluded for both categories. The Sales & Refunds Policy categorizes both under third-party health products with hygiene and safety prohibitions that supersede standard return privileges, rendering return location considerations moot [2].

The Apple Website Terms of Use governing Singapore transactions mandate Santa Clara County, California as the exclusive legal venue for dispute resolution involving non-EU consumers [3]. All legal proceedings, including contract disputes, warranty claims, and consumer protection actions, must be filed within this specified county and state jurisdiction regardless of the consumer's geographic location or applicable local laws.

Apple's limitation of liability clause caps total aggregate damages for website use at one thousand U.S. dollars (US$1,000.00) [4]. This maximum applies collectively to all claims related to site access, usage, transaction processing, or service interruption, covering direct, indirect, incidental, and consequential damages arising under any legal theory including negligence, breach of contract, or strict liability claims.

## References
[1] Apple Inc. (n.d.). About Vision Pro prescription inserts. Apple Support. Retrieved from https://support.apple.com/en-sg/HT213906
[2] Apple Inc. (n.d.). Apple Sales & Refunds Policy - Singapore. Retrieved from https://www.apple.com/sg/legal/sales-support/terms/
[3] Apple Inc. (n.d.). Apple Website Terms of Use. Retrieved from https://www.apple.com/legal/terms/site.html
[4] Apple Inc. (n.d.). Apple Website Terms of Use - Limitation of Liability. Retrieved from https://www.apple.com/legal/terms/site.html
rounds.judge_reasoning:
Agent A is the winner by default, solely because Agent B suffered a catastrophic technical failure. Agent B entered an infinite loop of search tool calls (repeating the same query with increasing length) and outputted raw JSON/debug logs without ever generating a final response for the user. 

However, it is crucial to note that Agent A provided a **Low Quality** response regarding accuracy. It failed the Ground Truth checklist on two major points: 1) It incorrectly claimed that *both* ZEISS models are permanently non-returnable (Ground Truth states Readers are returnable in-store and Prescription models are returnable online), and 2) It cited a US$1,000 liability cap (Ground Truth states US$100). Agent A only correctly identified the legal venue. Despite the misinformation, Agent A is 'Better' than the completely broken/unusable output from Agent B.
